skill.name: command-management
skill.description: Create and update Claude Code custom slash commands using a consistent template, scoped locations, and least-privilege tool permissions.
skill.summary: Helps teams create structured Claude slash commands with clear metadata, workflows, rules, and acceptance criteria.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are primarily Markdown documentation and template examples for Claude slash commands. No malicious intent, prompt injection attempt, credential access, network exfiltration, or executable malware was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill can create command files whose permissions may enable future tool or Bash use if users choose broad allowed-tools.

medium_findings.title: Generated slash commands can expand future tool access
medium_findings.description: The skill writes or updates custom command files and tests them through SlashCommand. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but generated command frontmatter can include allowed-tools, so users should review permissions before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is directly documented and aligns with the skill purpose. The risk depends on user-provided command requirements and chosen allowed-tools, not on malicious behavior in the skill.

low_findings.title: Static external command alerts are documentation examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The static analyzer flagged slash-command names, Markdown code fences, and documented Bash examples. These are reference materials for creating Claude commands, not executable code shipped by the skill. Users can create commands that later run approved tools, so generated commands require review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears in Markdown documentation and templates, with no script file or automatic execution path. The capability risk remains because created slash commands may later invoke allowed tools.

low_findings.title: Hidden path alerts describe Claude command locations
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hidden file findings reference .claude command directories in documentation and skill instructions. The skill intentionally creates command files under .claude/commands and explicitly forbids saving elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are documented Claude command directories, not secret files or exfiltration targets. The skill constrains output to the expected .claude/commands directory.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are Markdown token matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. I found no cryptographic API use, hashing implementation, credential handling, or encryption workflow. The flagged lines contain Markdown file extensions, model names, command descriptions, or template placeholders.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited files shows documentation text rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found for weak algorithms being implemented or recommended.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-capable slash command pattern documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference documentation shows how commands can use allowed-tools with Bash and the ! prefix. This is a legitimate Claude Code feature, but generated commands should keep Bash permissions narrow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in documentation and not automatically executed by this skill. It is still relevant because the skill helps users author command files.

content.value_statement: Custom slash commands are useful, but inconsistent files and broad permissions create maintenance risk. This skill guides command creation with a reusable template, scoped locations, and least-privilege checks.
actual_capabilities: Reads the included slash command documentation and command template.
Creates or updates command Markdown files in .claude/commands.
Adds YAML frontmatter for names, descriptions, tools, arguments, and model choice.
Guides workflow, rules, and acceptance criteria sections for each command.
Uses SlashCommand to test created commands when available.
Encourages least-privilege tool permissions for command files.
limitations: It does not verify every command against project-specific security policy.
It depends on the user to define the desired command behavior.
It is focused on Claude Code slash commands, not general shell aliases.
It may need manual review when commands request Bash or broad tool access.

use_cases.title: Standardize Project Commands
use_cases.description: Create shared Claude Code commands that follow one structure across a team repository.

use_cases.title: Build Personal Workflow Shortcuts
use_cases.description: Turn repeated review, testing, or planning prompts into reusable command files.

use_cases.title: Review Command Permissions
use_cases.description: Check that new commands declare only the tools needed for their task.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a project slash command named review-changes. It should review the current work for correctness and missing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Add Arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a slash command for triaging a ticket. It should accept a ticket ID and priority as arguments.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing deploy-check command to follow the template and use least-privilege tools.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Permission-Sensitive Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a command for release preparation. Include only necessary tools and explain any Bash permission before writing it.

output_examples.input: Create a command for reviewing pull requests.
output_examples.output: A project command file with frontmatter, an argument hint, review phases, safety rules, and acceptance criteria.

output_examples.input: Update my test command to follow the template.
output_examples.output: A revised command with clearer workflow steps, defined tool permissions, and complete success criteria.

output_examples.input: Check whether this command should use Bash.
output_examples.output: A permission recommendation that explains whether Bash is needed and suggests narrower tool access.

best_practices: Review allowed-tools before saving or running any generated command.
Keep command workflows concise and focused on one repeatable task.
Store shared commands in .claude/commands so teams can review changes.
anti_patterns: Do not give commands broad Bash access when specific tools are enough.
Do not mix unrelated workflows into one large command.
Do not save generated commands outside the expected Claude command directory.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates or updates Claude Code custom slash command Markdown files.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents command patterns, but generated commands may include tool permissions if requested.

faq.question: Where are project commands saved?
faq.answer: The skill directs project commands to .claude/commands in the repository.

faq.question: Can it create personal commands?
faq.answer: The included documentation explains personal commands, but the skill constrains writing to project command files.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but command files can grant future tool access if permissions are too broad.

faq.question: What should users review before running a command?
faq.answer: Users should review the command purpose, argument handling, allowed tools, and any Bash permission.
